blocks.text: Chapter 1.
The Herd Inside Your Head

Come closer to the fire, friend — new book tonight, and this one is a field guide to your own mind's traps. Rolf Dobelli collected ninety-nine ways smart people fool themselves, and we will walk them in eight nights, a handful of traps per night, each with the famous experiment that exposed it.

But before the first trap, a warning about all of them: none of these are stupidity. Every error in this book is an old survival tool, built for the savannah, still firing in the supermarket. You cannot remove them. You can only learn their names — because a named trap is a visible trap.
Tonight's five all come from one place: other people. There is a herd living inside your head, friend. Let me show you it running.
The lines that bent thirty percent of eyes

Picture a psychology lab in the 1950s. Solomon Asch shows a room of young men a card with one line on it, then a second card with three lines — one obviously the same length, the other two obviously not. Child's play. Alone, people get it right essentially every time.
But in this room, only one man is a real subject. The rest are actors — and one by one, calmly, unanimously, they all give the same wrong answer. Then it's the real subject's turn.
Pause and guess: He can see the lines with his own eyes. The answer is not ambiguous. What do you think he does?
Ready? Here is what happened.

On roughly a third of the trials, he goes along with the crowd — denying the plain evidence of his own eyes rather than stand alone against a unanimous room. That is social proof: the feeling that behaving like everyone else is behaving correctly. And it isn't weakness — it's ancestral wisdom. On the savannah, when the whole tribe suddenly ran, the ones who stopped to personally verify the tiger left the gene pool. Copy-the-herd kept your ancestors alive for fifty thousand years. Today the same reflex is farmed commercially: canned laughter to make jokes land, manufactured queues outside nightclubs, "bestseller" stickers that sell books regardless of what's in them, and stock bubbles where the only evidence anyone holds is that everyone else is buying. Dobelli's counter-maxim deserves memorizing: even if fifty million people say a foolish thing, it is still foolish. Popularity is evidence of popularity — nothing more.
The room where nobody spoke
Now put the herd inside a team of brilliant people, close the door, and watch what it becomes.

In 1961, President Kennedy's advisers — some of the sharpest minds in America — approved a plan to land 1,400 exiles on a Cuban beach called the Bay of Pigs. The plan failed on every single assumption; the promised mountain escape route even turned out to lie across eighty miles of impassable swamp. Here is the haunting part, uncovered by psychologist Irving Janis: almost every adviser had private doubts. And none of them spoke.

That is groupthink — social proof in a closed room. Each person reads the others' silence as confidence, censors their own doubt, and the group ends up believing something no individual member fully believes. Cohesion makes it worse: in a team that feels like family, dissent feels like betrayal. Dobelli adds Swissair — an airline so drunk on its own consensus that contrary voices about its expansion simply never surfaced, until the company fell out of the sky financially.

The fix is structural, never attitudinal: appoint a devil's advocate on purpose, have the most senior person speak last — and if you sit in a unanimous room holding a doubt, understand that voicing it is not disloyalty. It is the one job the group cannot do without you.

And teams hide a quieter leak: social loafing. Ringelmann's rope experiments found two people each pull at 93% of their solo strength; eight people, at about half. Where individual effort is invisible, everyone eases off — so make each contribution visible, and beware decisions where responsibility belongs to "the team," which is to say, to no one.
The man in the white coat
The herd has a shepherd, too — and your brain obeys him even faster than it obeys the crowd. His costume is a white coat, a uniform, a title on a door.

Pause and guess: Stanley Milgram, 1961: volunteers were told by a calm scientist in a lab coat to deliver electric shocks — rising in intensity — to a screaming stranger in the next room (an actor, but they didn't know that). The dial went to 450 volts, marked danger. Before you hear the answer, guess: what fraction of ordinary people went all the way?
Ready? Here is what happened.

About two thirds. Not sadists — teachers, salesmen, engineers, sweating and protesting and continuing, because a man with a clipboard said "the experiment requires that you continue." Milgram had designed the study to understand how ordinary Germans could have participated in the Holocaust; his answer was that authority alone suffices. That is authority bias — and Dobelli's twist is that authority earns this deference far less than we assume: economists as a profession missed the 2008 crisis, and doctors bled patients for centuries. Yet one industry actually beat the bias: aviation. After crashes where junior officers saw the captain's error and stayed politely silent, airlines built crew resource management — explicit training that requires juniors to challenge and captains to invite it. Accident rates fell. So judge the argument, not the epaulettes; ask for the expert's track record, not their title — and if you are the authority in the room, remember that your team's silence is not agreement. It's Milgram's dial, turning.
The flower that wasn't free

In the 1970s, travelers hurrying through American airports were intercepted by smiling devotees who pressed a flower into their hands. A gift — no charge! Most people didn't want the flower. Many binned it thirty steps later. And donations to the sect soared — until airports banned the practice.

That is reciprocity: the deep compulsion to repay what we're given, even gifts we never asked for. Like every trap tonight, it began as survival — the hunter who shared surplus meat was banking meals against the day his own hunt failed; reciprocity built cooperation, trade, civilization itself. Which is exactly why you can't switch it off, and why commerce quietly runs on it: the supermarket free sample that turns tasters into indebted buyers, the charity envelope with its "gift" of address labels, the vendor's box seats and conference dinners — never called a bribe, always invoiced later as goodwill.

Dobelli's rule: when someone hands you something free, ask what it will cost you. If you can't afford the obligation, decline the flower — or take it while consciously refusing the debt that came stapled to it.
The salesman who likes you

Joe Girard, once the world's most successful car salesman, had a method of beautiful simplicity: every month, every customer got a card in the mail. It said, in effect, I like you. That's it. That was the machine.

Liking bias: we buy from, concede to, and believe people we like — and research says likeability has three manufacturable triggers: they're attractive, they're similar to us, or they signal they like us. Every one can be faked at scale. The mirrored posture, the discovered shared hobby — "you golf too?" — the flattery that works even when you know it's flattery. Tupperware built an empire on the purest version: the seller at a Tupperware party is your friend or neighbor, so refusing the plastic feels like refusing the friendship. Even charity runs the trigger — one appealing child's face outraises a statistic about thousands, which is why money flows to the likeable needy, not the neediest.

The defense is a small, cold ritual: evaluate every offer as if it came from an unlikeable stranger — would you sign if this arrived by anonymous email? A good product survives that test. A good salesperson's product often does not.
Before you go

Five traps, one thread: your brain treats people as evidence — the crowd's behavior, the room's silence, the coat's authority, the giver's gift, the seller's smile — when none of them are evidence of anything except themselves. The counter-move is always the same separation: the message from the messenger, the deal from the giver, your own eyes from the crowd's.

But tomorrow night's traps are stranger — because they don't need other people at all. They live in your wallet. Why finishing a doomed project feels responsible. Why losing fifty rupees hurts twice as much as winning it delights. Why the mug you own is worth double the identical mug you don't. Money, it turns out, bends thinking like gravity bends light.
Next time: how money warps judgment — sunk costs, loss aversion, and the trap that built the Concorde.
Key takeaways
Social proof: popularity is evidence of popularity, nothing more — fifty million people can still be wrong.
Groupthink: silence is not agreement; appoint a devil's advocate and let the senior voice speak last.
Social loafing: invisible effort shrinks — make individual contributions visible.
Authority bias: judge the argument, not the epaulettes; ask for track records, and make dissent safe when you hold the authority.
Reciprocity: every free gift ships with an invisible invoice — decline it or consciously refuse the debt.
Liking bias: re-evaluate every offer as if it came from an unlikeable stranger; a good deal survives the test.
